item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got here to ask exactly the same. Did I miss something or just needs to be fixed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does -18 dig at the mountain for reemergence, but -9 is the one that disappears?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I chewed on this for a bit and decided I liked it. Good "alien from our own planet" stuff.
Though it must be noted that "organism" is misspelled in the main description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Well, they are non cellular organisms consisting of essentially only crystal, rock, and magma, which is pretty anomalous. The foundation contains far more mundane creatures like 098 and 236, which use regular cells and biological processes.

2. Yeah, I was going for a beached whales kinda thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I enjoy this piece, but I don't actually see why these creatures would be hidden by the Foundation.

Unless they aren't naturally occuring on Earth, they're really just mundane, if highly unusual, animals, and probably don't warrant attempted containment or occlusion.

Also, the presence of defence mechanisms suggest that there are predatory lifeforms, or unique hazards, in the Earth's mantel. Magma expulsion implies retreat, and crying implies parental care after birth. Is that your intention?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry if this is a bit of a necropost, but yeah, essentially, although the starfish is more of a cone with fins. I'm considering a rewrite in the summer when I have more time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm imagining large starfish shaped, rigid noodle like creatures made of quartz plating. Am I on the right track here? While I like the idea, it just feels like something is missing. It feels incomplete and very bare bones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Didn't see this post for a long time, so sorry if I'm a bit late. But yes, that's the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the SCPs that rush towards magma vents are like beaching whales?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, they're separate. Maybe they're different parts of the ecosystem down in the mantle, or came from similar evolutionary paths, but overall, they're different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So these are those big worms involved the scp about the soldiers who got sucked underground??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I've added some more stuff. Is it good, or just more fluff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have an idea to add to this, a new angle. Wait until I get home, at about 4:00

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although they use magma as blood, since their original environment is entirely comprised of it they can just induct more. The only time they use their own blood is when they are desperate, such as when they are above the crust.

Besides that, I'll try to edit some more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some issues I would like to address:

  • If the creature treats magma as its blood, why does it eject magma at high pressures for locomotion? It feels counter-productive to be bleeding just by moving about.
  • I'm somewhat confused. Does these creatures cause the eruption (that expelled them out of the volcano) or are the eruptions random occurrences?

I have mixed feelings about this article. It's trying to tell the readers something, though it feels lacking at the moment. Why do these creatures end up in the surface when it is likely it can't survive up there? There's many ways to go for this, but neutral vote for the current revision of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would actually argue that articles like that could succeed given that the object or creature in question is interesting enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These things are just kind of there. There's nothing interesting about a lava monster just on its own, Series 3 articles need to be more than just an object or a creature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm close to liking this one, but I'm a no-vote for now. There are some tone issues that I will let others with more time analyze, but for me the real hurdle is its missing that ever-elusive hook that takes something from good to great. Find something that makes these things more than just lava monsters. I will say that you're on the right track with the Euclid thing- more dangerous is not necessarily more interesting. I'd suggest that you might expand a little on the documentary side of things: what do we know about their life cycles, do they nest anywhere, are they important to plate tectonics or the health of the earth's mantle and core (and thus the magnetosphere)? There's a lot you can play with here.

item.title: Re: not contained?
item.content:encoded: Well, since they die roughly 24 hours after emergence and are in the middle of an active volcano, there's not really much the foundation needs to do to prevent civilian contact.

item.title: not contained?
item.content:encoded:
it's a neat idea, but your special containment procedures are kind of lacking. you're essentially saying, "yeah, we don't have any control over them whatsoever. just be on the lookout."

the description also left me wanting more. but that's a good thing! i really like the idea, so i want you to flesh it out more so I can enjoy it with a more satisfying read
